steps.canonical_text: Suspect anaphylaxis if there is swollen face, swollen lips, or a swollen tongue, trouble breathing, a tight throat, dizziness, or repeated vomiting after a likely trigger.
Call emergency services now and say it is a severe allergic reaction. Anaphylaxis can worsen within minutes.
If they have a prescribed epinephrine auto-injector, help them use it in the outer thigh now as directed on the device. It can go through clothing.

asset_refs.caption: Use the person's prescribed epinephrine auto-injector
asset_refs.content_description: Two prescribed epinephrine auto-injector devices shown side by side.

asset_refs.caption: Place the auto-injector against the outer thigh
asset_refs.content_description: A responder positioning an epinephrine auto-injector against the outer thigh of a seated child.

asset_refs.caption: Press and hold the injector in the outer thigh as directed
asset_refs.content_description: A close-up of an epinephrine auto-injector pressed into the outer thigh during use.

steps.canonical_text: Lay them flat and raise their legs if possible. If they are struggling to breathe, let them sit up. If vomiting or unconscious but breathing, place them on their side. Do not let them suddenly stand up.
must_keep_keywords: Do not let them suddenly stand up

steps.canonical_text: If symptoms do not improve after about 5 minutes and a second auto-injector is available, give the second dose.
Stay with them and keep checking breathing and alertness. If they become unresponsive and stop breathing normally, start chest compressions.
